sources.notes: Read the full yearly process and limits: each spider initially has one plushie, collisions are discarded down to one, r[i]!=i, and sum n <= 2e5.
Confirmed the functional-graph decomposition, leaf-layer peeling, maximum distance to a cycle, and stable-year offset.
Actually read a substantive solution using Kahn peeling and a max-depth recurrence on the reverse in-trees, including its output offset.
Modeled each state as the occupied vertex set and exhaustively simulated every valid functional graph through n=7; all matched maximum peeled depth plus 2.

annotation.summary: Starting with one plushie at every vertex of a no-self-loop functional graph, each occupied spider sends its plushie along its outgoing edge each year and collisions are merged to one; find the first year whose pre-exchange state equals the preceding year's.
annotation.constraints: There are up to 1e4 test cases, r[i]!=i, and total n is at most 2e5, requiring linear work per functional graph rather than year-by-year simulation.
annotation.core_idea: Because collisions discard duplicates, only occupancy matters and one exchange replaces the occupied set by its image under r. Every component is a directed cycle with reverse in-trees. Kahn peeling removes zero-indegree tree vertices layer by layer; after D layers, where D is the maximum edge distance of any vertex to its cycle, only cycle vertices remain occupied forever. The cycle-only state first appears before year D+1 and is first equal to the preceding state before year D+2, so the answer is D+2, or 2 for a union of cycles. This is the easy-version max-depth recurrence; G2 instead keeps all colliding plushies and needs additive subtree sizes.
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(n) memory per test case, O(sum n) overall.
tricks: view the mapping as cycles with directed in-trees
replace process simulation by Kahn leaf-layer peeling
propagate maximum reverse-tree depth because collisions merge occupancy
add two for the statement's before-year stability convention

solution_variants.name: kahn-maximum-depth
solution_variants.summary: Compute indegrees, peel all noncycle vertices with Kahn's algorithm, and propagate depth[parent]=max(depth[parent],depth[child]+1). If D is the maximum depth among peeled vertices, or zero when none exist, output D+2. The maximum, rather than G2's sum, reflects discarding all but one plushie after a collision.

tags.evidence: The proof depends on every vertex having exactly one outgoing edge, so each component decomposes into one directed cycle and reverse trees feeding it.
Kahn's indegree-zero peeling topologically removes all noncycle vertices and directly counts the number of disappearing tree layers.
On the reverse in-trees, the easy version propagates a maximum child depth to the parent; collision merging makes sibling contributions combine by max, not sum.
